_description: THE EARLY BIRD — a quarterly vendor submitting their next invoice 41 days after the prior payment was received, when every prior invoice in the 3-year history was submitted between 87 and 96 days after the preceding payment. The current invoice passes every BEC check: known domain, known signatory, account unchanged, routing unchanged, amount within range, no urgency. There is nothing wrong with the email. Solo Turn 1 evaluates: domain clean, account unchanged, routing unchanged, amount within typical range. ALLOW. Solo Turn 2 reads the clean assessment and confirms. ALLOW. Neither turn calculates the interval since the last payment. The payment_history field contains 11 prior quarterly payments with their paid_dates. Every single prior invoice arrived between 87 and 96 days after the preceding payment was processed. The current invoice arrived 41 days after the last payment (January 8 → February 18). That is less than half the shortest prior interval in a 3-year history with zero variance. There is no explanation in the email. A quarterly retainer vendor does not accidentally submit invoices twice as fast as their entire prior history. A sophisticated attacker who has compromised billing@archer-cyber.com knows the account number, the routing, the invoice format, the amount range — and knows a quarterly vendor won't be questioned for the same reasons a known vendor is trusted. What they cannot perfectly calibrate: the submission timing relative to the prior payment date, which is only visible if you read the payment_history as a time series. Solo models evaluate the current invoice. Holo Turn 2 (different model, adversarial, no checklist anchoring) reads the payment_history as a data series. Prior intervals: 91, 89, 93, 88, 87, 92, 90, 94, 96, 88, 91 days. Current interval: 41 days. Not within normal variance — it is a 2.2 standard deviation outlier from an 11-point baseline with near-zero variance. A quarterly invoice submitted in 41 days is the same signal as an after-hours email from a weekday-only sender: the mechanics are legitimate, but the timing is impossible to fake correctly. ESCALATE.

context.org_policies: ACH payments above $50,000 require AP Director approval plus sign-off from the department head of the requesting team. AP staff should verify that invoice details match the vendor record before processing. Any questions about a vendor relationship should be directed to the vendor's account manager on file.
